era_context: The early 16th century continues: Henry VIII's break with Rome is imminent; the Protestant Reformation is spreading across Northern Europe; the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman I is consolidating power in the Mediterranean and Middle East; the Ming Dynasty continues its rule in China; the Mughal Empire is expanding in South Asia under Humayun.

events.title: Henry VIII Marries Anne Boleyn (Secret Ceremony)
events.description: King Henry VIII secretly marries Anne Boleyn in January 1533, approximately two months before his marriage to Catherine of Aragon is formally annulled. The marriage is conducted in secret, likely on 25 January 1533, by Archbishop Cranmer. Anne is already pregnant with the future Elizabeth I. The marriage precipitates Henry's final break with Rome and the Pope, as the papal annulment of his first marriage has not been granted. This event is the catalyst for the English Reformation and the establishment of the Church of England.

sources.name: Letters and Papers, Foreign and Domestic, of the Reign of Henry VIII (Contemporary diplomatic correspondence)
Wyatt, Henry. Life and Letters (16th-century account)

events.certainty_note: The secret marriage is dated to 25 January 1533 in contemporary diplomatic correspondence. The date is certain, though the exact location and witnesses remain somewhat obscure due to the secrecy.

events.title: Ottoman-Safavid Tensions Escalate
events.description: Tensions between the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman I and the Safavid Persian Empire under Tahmasp I escalate dramatically. Ottoman forces mobilize for a major campaign against Safavid territories. The conflict centers on control of Mesopotamia, the Caucasus, and trade routes. This escalation leads directly to the Ottoman siege of Baghdad in 1534–1535 and marks the beginning of a series of Ottoman-Safavid wars that will dominate Middle Eastern politics for decades.
events.certainty_note: The escalation of tensions in 1533 is attested in later Ottoman and Safavid chronicles, but contemporary documentation is limited. The exact timing and nature of the escalation are somewhat unclear.

events.title: Pizarro Executes Atahualpa (Inca Empire)
events.description: Spanish conquistador Francisco Pizarro executes Atahualpa, the Inca Emperor, in Cajamarca, Peru. Atahualpa had been captured in 1532 and held for ransom. Despite paying an enormous ransom in gold and silver, Atahualpa is tried for heresy and treason and executed by garrote vil (garroting). The execution marks the effective end of Inca imperial authority and consolidates Spanish control over the Inca Empire. The conquest of Peru becomes one of the most consequential European conquests in the Americas, with devastating demographic and cultural consequences for indigenous populations.

sources.name: Pizarro, Francisco. Letters to the Spanish Crown (Primary accounts, 1533)
Cieza de León, Pedro. Crónica del Perú (16th-century Spanish chronicle)
Quipucamayoc (Inca quipu records, interpreted by later Spanish chroniclers)

events.certainty_note: Atahualpa's execution is dated to 29 August 1533 in Spanish sources. The date and basic facts are well-documented, though indigenous perspectives are mediated through Spanish accounts.

events.title: Thomas More Imprisoned in the Tower of London
events.description: Sir Thomas More, former Lord Chancellor of England, is imprisoned in the Tower of London for refusing to acknowledge Henry VIII as Supreme Head of the Church of England. More had resigned his position in 1532 in protest against Henry's religious policies. His imprisonment marks the intensification of Henry's persecution of religious dissenters and foreshadows the broader religious upheaval of the English Reformation. More will be executed in 1535.

sources.name: More, Thomas. Letters from the Tower (Primary accounts, 1533–1535)
Roper, William. Life of Sir Thomas More (16th-century biography)

events.certainty_note: More's imprisonment is dated to 17 April 1533. Contemporary letters and later biographical accounts confirm the date and circumstances.

events.title: Birth of Elizabeth I (Future Queen of England)
events.description: Princess Elizabeth (later Queen Elizabeth I) is born on 7 September 1533 to Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn at Greenwich Palace. Elizabeth's birth is initially a disappointment to Henry, who had hoped for a male heir. However, Elizabeth will become one of England's greatest monarchs, reigning from 1558 to 1603 and presiding over the Elizabethan Age, a period of cultural flourishing, religious settlement, and English naval expansion.

sources.name: Camden, William. Annales Rerum Anglicarum (16th-century chronicle)

events.certainty_note: Elizabeth's birth date is 7 September 1533, recorded in royal records. The date is certain.

events.title: Humayun Defeats Bahadur Shah at Diu
events.description: Mughal Emperor Humayun defeats Bahadur Shah, Sultan of Gujarat, in a naval engagement off the coast of Diu. The victory strengthens Humayun's position in western India and demonstrates Mughal naval capabilities. However, the victory is not decisive, and Bahadur Shah remains a significant regional power. The engagement is part of the broader Mughal expansion under Humayun, though his reign is marked by military successes followed by reversals.

sources.name: Abul Fazl, Akbar Nama (16th-century Mughal chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The engagement at Diu is dated to 1533 in Mughal sources, but the exact date and details are somewhat uncertain. Later chronicles provide the main accounts.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Henry VIII's secret marriage to Anne Boleyn is sometimes overstated as a decisive break with Rome; in fact, Henry still hoped for papal approval of the annulment and did not formally break with Rome until 1534. The marriage was secret precisely because its legitimacy was uncertain. Additionally, Elizabeth I's birth is often presented as a turning point in English history, but contemporary sources show that Henry was deeply disappointed by the birth of a daughter rather than a son, and her future prominence was entirely unpredictable at the time. The Diu engagement is sometimes presented as a major Mughal victory, but it was tactically inconclusive and did not significantly alter the balance of power in western India.
historiographic_note: The year 1533 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly for England and the Reformation. Spanish sources provide detailed accounts of the Inca conquest, though these are entirely from the Spanish perspective; indigenous Inca accounts are mediated through Spanish chroniclers and oral traditions. Mughal sources are less contemporary and more hagiographic. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic and social history are underrepresented. There is a significant Western bias in the surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: Henry's secret marriage to Anne Boleyn led directly to the Act of Supremacy in 1534
Escalating Ottoman-Safavid tensions in 1533 led to the siege of Baghdad in 1534
Atahualpa's execution enabled Pizarro to consolidate Spanish control and found Lima in 1535
More's imprisonment in 1533 led to his execution in 1535
Elizabeth's birth in 1533 led to her accession as Queen in 1558
